skills.description: Many different Native American societies lived across North America before Columbus arrived
Geography and the environment shaped how different Native American cultures developed
The Pueblo peoples were settled farming societies in the American Southwest
Cahokia and other mound-building societies in the Mississippi Valley were advanced civilizations
Great Plains peoples were nomadic and depended on hunting to survive
Eastern Woodland peoples survived by combining farming with hunting and fishing
Pacific Northwest societies depended on fishing and had access to plentiful natural resources
Native Americans had different types of government, from small bands to large confederacies
The Haudenosaunee (Iroquois) Confederacy was an example of advanced Native political organization
Native American spiritual beliefs focused on a deep connection to the natural world
Europeans explored the Americas for wealth, to spread Christianity, and to compete with rival nations
Spain's history of the Reconquista and Catholic missionary goals drove their exploration
New technology like the caravel, compass, and joint-stock companies made exploration possible
Cortés conquered the Aztec Empire for Spain
Pizarro conquered the Inca Empire for Spain
Disease, better weapons, and Native allies helped Spain conquer these empires
The Columbian Exchange was the transfer of plants, animals, and diseases between the Americas and Europe
Old World diseases devastated Native American populations
New World crops like corn and potatoes changed diets and boosted populations in Europe
Europeans brought horses, cattle, and pigs to the Americas
Horses completely changed the way Great Plains Native Americans lived
The encomienda system was a form of forced Native labor under Spanish rule
The casta system was a racial hierarchy in Spanish colonies
As Native populations died off, colonizers brought more enslaved Africans to do the labor
French colonization focused on the fur trade and building alliances with Native Americans
Dutch colonization was centered on trade in the Hudson River Valley
The French partnered with Natives for trade, which was very different from how Spain conquered them
European colonization disrupted Native American societies and political systems
Native Americans resisted European colonization and attempts to change their culture
Europeans, Natives, and Africans all exchanged cultural practices and adapted to each other
The Spanish mission system was used to convert Natives to Christianity and control them
England colonized America for economic opportunity, religious freedom, and rivalry with other nations
Jamestown (1607) was the first permanent English settlement in America
Joint-stock companies like the Virginia Company funded English colonization
Tobacco became Virginia's main cash crop and created a huge demand for labor
The headright system gave land to people who paid for workers to come to Virginia
The Chesapeake colonies gradually shifted from using indentured servants to enslaved Africans
The Separatist Pilgrims founded Plymouth Colony in 1620
The Puritans founded Massachusetts Bay Colony, aiming to build a 'city upon a hill'
Puritan religious beliefs heavily influenced how New England was governed and organized
The Mayflower Compact was an early example of colonists governing themselves
Town meetings were a form of direct democracy in New England
Roger Williams was kicked out of Massachusetts and founded Rhode Island with religious tolerance
Anne Hutchinson challenged Puritan authority in the Antinomian Controversy
The New England, Middle, and Southern colonies had major differences from each other
New England's economy was based on trade, fishing, shipbuilding, and small farms
The Middle Colonies were ethnically diverse, grew lots of grain, and had mixed economies
The Southern Colonies' economy was built on cash crops and plantation farming
William Penn founded Pennsylvania as a Quaker colony with religious tolerance
Plantation farming in the South created an ever-growing demand for labor
Race-based chattel slavery developed in the British colonies, treating enslaved people as property
The Middle Passage was the brutal forced transport of enslaved Africans across the Atlantic
Slave codes were laws that defined enslaved people as property and took away their rights
Enslaved Africans kept their cultural traditions alive and formed their own communities
Enslaved people resisted through work slowdowns, running away, and outright rebellion
The Stono Rebellion (1739) was a major slave uprising in South Carolina
Bacon's Rebellion (1676) changed labor systems and deepened racial divisions in Virginia
Mercantilism was the economic system that governed British colonial trade
The Navigation Acts were British laws that restricted who the colonies could trade with
The triangular trade connected Europe, Africa, and the Americas in a trading network
English colonists and Native Americans had many interactions and conflicts
The Anglo-Powhatan Wars were early conflicts between colonists and Natives in Virginia
The Pequot War was a colonial-Native conflict in New England in the 1630s
King Philip's War (Metacom's War) was a devastating war between colonists and Natives
Colonial expansion increasingly pushed Native Americans off their land and to the margins
Colonial society and culture developed in unique ways in British North America
The First Great Awakening was a religious revival that challenged established church authority
Jonathan Edwards and George Whitefield were key figures in the Great Awakening
The Great Awakening encouraged individual thinking and questioning traditional authority
Enlightenment ideas spread through an Atlantic intellectual community connecting America and Europe
Key Enlightenment ideas included natural rights, reason, and the social contract
Salutary neglect was Britain's practice of loosely enforcing colonial rules
Because Britain didn't enforce rules strictly, the colonies developed traditions of self-government
Colonial assemblies like the Virginia House of Burgesses were important self-governing institutions
The Seven Years' War started because of rivalry between empires and disputes over colonial land
The British and colonists fought against the French and their Native American allies
Benjamin Franklin proposed the Albany Plan of Union to get the colonies to cooperate
Britain won the Seven Years' War, and the Treaty of Paris (1763) confirmed it
The Proclamation of 1763 banned colonial settlement west of the Appalachian Mountains
Britain's massive war debt led them to start taxing the colonies
The Stamp Act (1765) was a direct tax on printed materials in the colonies
The Townshend Acts put taxes on imported goods like tea and glass
Colonists argued there should be 'no taxation without representation' in Parliament
Colonists resisted through boycotts, petitions, the Stamp Act Congress, and groups like the Sons of Liberty
The Boston Massacre (1770) was used as anti-British propaganda
The Boston Tea Party (1773) was a protest against the Tea Act
The Intolerable (Coercive) Acts were Britain's punishment of Massachusetts
The First Continental Congress (1774) organized the colonies' response to British actions
Enlightenment philosophy influenced American revolutionary thinking
John Locke's philosophy centered on natural rights: life, liberty, and property
The social contract theory says government gets its authority from the consent of the people
Montesquieu came up with the idea of separation of powers between branches of government
Thomas Paine's 'Common Sense' convinced many colonists to support independence
The first shots of the Revolution were fired at Lexington and Concord in April 1775
The Second Continental Congress acted as the wartime government during the Revolution
The Declaration of Independence laid out principles of natural rights and self-governance
Thomas Jefferson was the main author of the Declaration of Independence
I know the key military events of the American Revolution
The Battle of Saratoga was the turning point that convinced France to ally with America
The French alliance was critical in helping America win the Revolution
George Washington commanded the Continental Army
The British surrendered at the Battle of Yorktown in 1781
The Treaty of Paris (1783) officially recognized American independence
Revolutionary ideals sparked debates about equality and who deserved rights
People debated the morality and future of slavery during the Revolution
The Revolution expanded some roles for women but also kept many limits in place
Republican Motherhood was the idea that women served the nation by raising good citizens
Northern states passed gradual emancipation laws after the Revolution
Some states expanded voting rights by lowering property requirements
The Articles of Confederation were America's first attempt at a national government
The Articles were weak — no power to tax, no executive branch, and amendments needed unanimous approval
Shays' Rebellion (1786-87) showed how weak the Articles of Confederation were
The Northwest Ordinance set up rules for governing new territories and admitting new states
The Constitutional Convention was called in Philadelphia in 1787 to fix the government
The Virginia Plan favored big states by basing representation on population
The New Jersey Plan favored small states by giving every state equal representation
The Great Compromise created a two-house legislature — the Senate and the House
The Three-Fifths Compromise counted enslaved people as 3/5 of a person for representation and taxes
The Electoral College is the system used to elect the president
Federalism divides power between the national government and state governments
The Constitution separates power among three branches: legislative, executive, and judicial
Checks and balances prevent any one branch of government from becoming too powerful
Federalists and Anti-Federalists debated whether to ratify the Constitution
Federalists argued for a strong national government in The Federalist Papers
James Madison, Alexander Hamilton, and John Jay wrote The Federalist Papers
Anti-Federalists worried the Constitution would create a tyrannical central government without a Bill of Rights
The Bill of Rights was added to protect individual freedoms and convince people to ratify the Constitution
The Bill of Rights protects freedom of speech, religion, press, the right to bear arms, and due process
George Washington's presidency set many important precedents for future presidents
Washington's Farewell Address warned against political parties and foreign alliances
The first two political parties were the Federalists and the Democratic-Republicans
Alexander Hamilton led the Federalists and favored industry, tariffs, and a national bank
Thomas Jefferson led the Democratic-Republicans and favored farming and a strict reading of the Constitution
Hamilton's financial plan included a national bank, taking on state debts, and excise taxes
People debated whether the national bank was constitutional — strict vs. loose interpretation
McCulloch v. Maryland upheld the national bank and established that federal law is supreme
The Whiskey Rebellion happened, and Washington sent troops to enforce federal law
The Jay Treaty (Britain) and Pinckney Treaty (Spain) were early foreign policy deals
The Alien and Sedition Acts under John Adams restricted free speech and immigration
The Virginia and Kentucky Resolutions argued states could nullify federal laws they saw as unconstitutional
The XYZ Affair led to an undeclared 'quasi-war' with France
The Election of 1800 was a peaceful transfer of power between opposing political parties
Jefferson's presidency focused on an agrarian republic with limited government
The Louisiana Purchase (1803) doubled the size of the United States
People debated whether Jefferson even had the constitutional power to buy Louisiana
Lewis and Clark explored the new western territory from the Louisiana Purchase
Marbury v. Madison (1803) established judicial review — courts can strike down unconstitutional laws
I know the causes and outcomes of the War of 1812
British impressment (forcing American sailors into the British navy) was a major cause of the War of 1812
Americans also wanted to expand into Canada and stop Native American resistance on the frontier
Tecumseh built a Native American confederacy to resist American westward expansion
The Hartford Convention was where Federalists met to oppose the War of 1812
The Federalist Party collapsed after the War of 1812
The Treaty of Ghent ended the War of 1812 with no territory changing hands
American nationalism and economic independence grew after the War of 1812
The Era of Good Feelings under President Monroe was a period of national unity
The Monroe Doctrine (1823) told European nations to stay out of the Western Hemisphere
Henry Clay's American System included protective tariffs, a national bank, and roads/canals
The Missouri Compromise (1820) kept the balance between free and slave states
The 36°30' line divided future territories into free (north) and slave (south)
The Market Revolution transformed the American economy in the early 1800s
The factory system started at Lowell, Massachusetts
New transportation — turnpikes, canals, steamboats, and railroads — changed the economy
The Erie Canal was a game-changer, connecting the Great Lakes to the Atlantic Ocean
The cotton gin made cotton production explode, which increased the demand for slave labor
A wage-labor economy grew in the North
The Lowell mill system relied on young women as factory workers
The Market Revolution made the economic differences between North and South even bigger
The Market Revolution caused major social and cultural changes
A middle class emerged along with the idea of 'separate spheres' — men in public life, women at home
Immigration from Ireland and Germany increased heavily in the 1830s and 1840s
Nativists opposed Catholic immigrants, leading to groups like the Know-Nothing Party
Democratic participation expanded during the Jacksonian era
Dropping property requirements allowed more white men to vote
Mass party politics and popular campaigning techniques rose during this era
Andrew Jackson was seen as a champion of the common man against wealthy elites
Jackson vetoed the recharter of the Second Bank of the United States
The Nullification Crisis was a showdown over federal tariffs — South Carolina tried to reject them
John C. Calhoun was the leading voice for nullification and Southern states' rights
Jackson forcefully shut down nullification, preserving federal authority
The Indian Removal Act (1830) led to the Trail of Tears — forced removal of Native Americans
Worcester v. Georgia ruled in favor of Cherokee sovereignty, but Jackson ignored the ruling
The Cherokee, Creek, Choctaw, and other nations were forcibly relocated from the Southeast
The Second Great Awakening was a massive religious revival movement
Revivalism promoted personal salvation and inspired people to push for social reforms
The abolitionist movement demanded an immediate end to slavery
William Lloyd Garrison published The Liberator, calling for the immediate end of slavery
Frederick Douglass was a formerly enslaved person who became a leading abolitionist
The Underground Railroad was a secret network that helped enslaved people escape to freedom
The women's rights movement held the Seneca Falls Convention in 1848
The Declaration of Sentiments was modeled after the Declaration of Independence
The temperance movement tried to reduce or ban alcohol
Horace Mann led the common school movement to expand public education
Utopian communities were experiments in creating ideal alternative societies
Transcendentalists like Emerson and Thoreau emphasized individualism and self-reliance
The plantation system in the South completely depended on enslaved labor
Enslaved people resisted through revolts, escapes, working slowly, and keeping their culture alive
Nat Turner's Rebellion (1831) was a major slave uprising in Virginia
Proslavery advocates defended slavery as a 'positive good' and even used the Bible to justify it
Slavery shaped the South's social structure, economy, and political power
Manifest Destiny was the belief that America was destined to expand across the entire continent
Texas was annexed (added to the US) in 1845 as part of westward expansion
The Oregon Treaty with Britain set the northern US border at the 49th parallel
Americans migrated west on the Oregon, California, and Santa Fe trails
I know the causes of the Mexican-American War (1846-1848)
The Treaty of Guadalupe Hidalgo gave a huge chunk of Mexican territory to the US
The Wilmot Proviso proposed banning slavery in any territory taken from Mexico
Gaining territory from Mexico reignited the fight over whether slavery would expand
The Compromise of 1850 tried to settle the slavery debate with several provisions
California was admitted as a free state under the Compromise of 1850
The Fugitive Slave Act required Northerners to help return escaped enslaved people
Popular sovereignty let territories like Utah and New Mexico vote on whether to allow slavery
The Fugitive Slave Act made Northerners even more opposed to slavery
Harriet Beecher Stowe's 'Uncle Tom's Cabin' turned public opinion against slavery
The North and South were growing further apart on major issues
The Northern economy was based on industry, free labor, and diverse farming
The Southern economy was based on plantations and enslaved labor
The North and South disagreed on tariffs, government-funded projects, and slavery expansion
The Kansas-Nebraska Act (1854) let new territories vote on slavery through popular sovereignty
The Kansas-Nebraska Act basically canceled the Missouri Compromise
Bleeding Kansas was a violent conflict between pro-slavery and anti-slavery settlers
The Republican Party formed to oppose the expansion of slavery
The Dred Scott decision said African Americans could not be citizens
Dred Scott also declared the Missouri Compromise unconstitutional
The Lincoln-Douglas debates focused on slavery, popular sovereignty, and the future of the Union
John Brown raided the federal arsenal at Harpers Ferry in 1859
Brown's raid deepened fears on both sides and pushed the country closer to war
Abraham Lincoln won the Election of 1860 on a platform against slavery's expansion
Lincoln's election caused Southern states to secede from the Union
Southern states formed the Confederate States of America
Jefferson Davis was the president of the Confederacy
I know the main events of the Civil War (1861-1865)
The North had advantages: more people, more factories, better railroads, and a stronger navy
The South had advantages: fighting on home turf, strong military leaders, and interior defensive lines
I know the key Civil War battles: Bull Run, Antietam, Gettysburg, Vicksburg, and Sherman's March
The Battle of Gettysburg was a major turning point of the Civil War
Grant's strategy was total war and wearing down the Confederacy through attrition
Lee surrendered to Grant at Appomattox Court House in April 1865, ending the war
The Emancipation Proclamation was issued in January 1863
The Emancipation Proclamation changed the purpose of the war to include ending slavery
African Americans served in the Union Army, including the famous 54th Massachusetts regiment
The government expanded during the war: military draft, income tax, Homestead Act, Morrill Act
Lincoln suspended habeas corpus (the right to a trial) during the Civil War
The Gettysburg Address redefined the war as a fight for equality and democracy
The 13th Amendment abolished slavery throughout the United States
Presidential Reconstruction under Lincoln and Johnson was relatively lenient toward the South
Lincoln's Ten Percent Plan made it easy for Southern states to rejoin the Union
Andrew Johnson continued lenient Reconstruction and pardoned many former Confederates
Radical Republicans opposed Johnson's soft approach to Reconstruction
Congressional Reconstruction put the South under military rule with the Reconstruction Acts
The 14th Amendment granted citizenship and guaranteed equal protection under the law
The 15th Amendment said voting rights can't be denied based on race
The Freedmen's Bureau provided education, food, and legal help to formerly enslaved people
African Americans participated in politics during Reconstruction
Black elected officials served in state and federal government during Reconstruction
The House of Representatives impeached President Andrew Johnson
White Southerners resisted Reconstruction through violence, intimidation, and restrictive laws
Black Codes were Southern laws designed to restrict the rights of formerly enslaved people
The KKK used terror and violence to stop Black people from participating in politics
Sharecropping and the crop-lien system trapped formerly enslaved people in cycles of debt
The Compromise of 1877 settled the disputed election and ended Reconstruction
Federal troops were pulled out of the South after Reconstruction ended
Jim Crow segregation and racial oppression took over the South after Reconstruction
Westward expansion and economic development accelerated after the Civil War
The Transcontinental Railroad was completed in 1869, connecting the East and West coasts
The Homestead Act encouraged settlement out West by offering free land
Railroads connected national markets and sped up economic growth
The cattle industry and ranching became a major part of the Great Plains economy
Mining booms in the West drew waves of new settlers
American settlers and Native Americans clashed violently in the West
The destruction of the buffalo herds devastated Plains Indian ways of life
The Indian Wars included the Battle of Little Bighorn and the Wounded Knee Massacre
The reservation system forced Native Americans onto restricted pieces of land
The Dawes Act (1887) broke up tribal lands into individual plots
The Dawes Act tried to force assimilation while destroying Native communal land traditions
The 'New South' promoted industrialization, but progress was limited
Sharecropping and tenant farming continued to dominate the Southern economy after the war
The South had some industrial growth, like textile mills and tobacco processing, but it was limited
Jim Crow laws required racial segregation across the South
Plessy v. Ferguson (1896) upheld 'separate but equal' segregation as constitutional
Black voters were kept from voting through poll taxes, literacy tests, and grandfather clauses
Lynching and racial violence were used as tools of terror against Black people in the South
Ida B. Wells was a leading journalist and activist who fought against lynching
Technological innovations drove the massive industrialization after the Civil War
Key inventions included the telephone, electric light, and the Bessemer steel process
New technologies transformed how businesses operated and how people lived daily life
Big business and industrial capitalism rose to dominate the Gilded Age economy
Carnegie (steel), Rockefeller (oil), and JP Morgan (finance) were major industrialists
Vertical and horizontal integration were strategies companies used to dominate industries
Trusts and monopolies formed to control entire industries
The Sherman Antitrust Act (1890) was the first federal attempt to regulate monopolies
The Gospel of Wealth and Social Darwinism were used to justify inequality
Laissez-faire economics and limited government defined Gilded Age policy
Industrial workers faced terrible conditions: long hours, low pay, and dangerous workplaces
Labor unions grew as workers organized to fight for better conditions
The Knights of Labor welcomed all workers, including unskilled laborers
The AFL under Samuel Gompers focused on skilled craft workers
Major strikes included the Great Railroad Strike (1877), Haymarket, Homestead, and Pullman
Government and business used courts, troops, and legal orders to crush labor unions
Mass immigration defined the Gilded Age
Immigration shifted from Northern/Western Europe to Southern/Eastern Europe
Chinese immigrants contributed significant labor, including building the Transcontinental Railroad
Immigrants in cities lived in ethnic neighborhoods and tenement housing while preserving their cultures
The Chinese Exclusion Act (1882) was the first major federal law restricting immigration
Nativist movements opposed immigration based on race, religion, and economic fears
American cities grew explosively during the Gilded Age
Cities faced overcrowding, poor sanitation, poverty, and political corruption
Political machines like Tammany Hall traded services for votes
Consumer culture and an urban middle class were growing
Public education, libraries, and new forms of entertainment expanded
Reform movements arose to tackle Gilded Age social and economic problems
The Social Gospel movement applied Christian values to fight urban poverty and inequality
Settlement houses like Jane Addams' Hull House served immigrant communities
The Populist movement represented farmers fighting against corporate power
Populists demanded free silver coinage, a graduated income tax, and railroad regulation
The People's Party and William Jennings Bryan ran for president on populist platforms
Bryan's 'Cross of Gold' speech called for silver currency and challenged the gold standard
Gilded Age politics were marked by corruption, patronage, and limited federal action
The spoils system was corrupt, and people pushed for civil service reform
The Pendleton Act (1883) established hiring federal workers based on merit instead of political connections
Tariff policy was a major ongoing debate during the Gilded Age
The gold standard debate was connected to bigger fights over monetary policy
Americans debated whether the US should become an imperial power in the 1890s
Arguments for expansion included new markets, Social Darwinism, naval strategy, and a 'civilizing mission'
Anti-imperialists said expansion violated democratic ideals and the Constitution
Alfred Thayer Mahan's book argued that sea power was the key to national greatness
I know the causes and events of the Spanish-American War (1898)
Yellow journalism (sensationalized news) helped build public support for war with Spain
After winning, the US took control of the Philippines, Puerto Rico, and Guam
The Platt Amendment gave the US the right to intervene in Cuba
The Philippine-American War was fought when Filipinos resisted US colonial rule
The Open Door Policy tried to ensure equal trading access for all nations in China
Teddy Roosevelt used 'Big Stick' diplomacy and built the Panama Canal
The Roosevelt Corollary said the US could act as a 'police force' in Latin America
The Progressive movement was a response to Gilded Age problems
Progressives wanted to regulate business, reduce corruption, expand democracy, and protect workers
Muckraking journalists exposed corruption, dangerous conditions, and social injustice
Key muckrakers: Upton Sinclair (The Jungle), Ida Tarbell (Standard Oil), and Jacob Riis
Teddy Roosevelt's Square Deal focused on trust-busting, consumer protection, and conservation
The Hepburn Act regulated railroads and the Pure Food and Drug Act protected consumers
Roosevelt was a conservationist who established national parks and forests
Woodrow Wilson's 'New Freedom' program focused on economic reform
The Federal Reserve Act (1913) created America's central banking system
The Clayton Antitrust Act gave the government more power to break up monopolies
The 16th Amendment allowed an income tax; the 17th allowed direct election of senators
Progressive reforms at the state level included the initiative, referendum, recall, and direct primary
The women's suffrage movement won the right to vote with the 19th Amendment (1920)
Progressivism largely failed to address racial equality for African Americans
Booker T. Washington pushed for vocational education and gradual economic progress for Black Americans
W.E.B. Du Bois pushed for immediate full civil rights and co-founded the NAACP
The 18th Amendment established Prohibition — banning alcohol
I know the causes of US entry into World War I
German submarine warfare and the Zimmermann Telegram pushed the US toward war
The US entered World War I in April 1917
American troops helped the Allies win on the Western Front
The WWI home front involved propaganda, food conservation, war bonds, and expanded government power
The Espionage Act, Sedition Act, and Schenck v. US restricted civil liberties during WWI
The Great Migration was the mass movement of Black Americans from the rural South to Northern cities
Women gained new job opportunities during World War I
Wilson's Fourteen Points laid out his vision for a fair and lasting peace
The Treaty of Versailles punished Germany harshly and created the League of Nations
The US Senate rejected the Treaty of Versailles and refused to join the League of Nations
1920s immigration was restricted by the Emergency Quota Act and National Origins Act
Nativism, racism, and eugenics influenced 1920s immigration policy
The KKK surged again in the 1920s, targeting immigrants, Catholics, and Jews
The 1920s were full of cultural and political tensions
The First Red Scare (1919-1920) and Palmer Raids targeted suspected radicals
The Scopes Trial (1925) showed the tension between modern science and religious fundamentalism
Prohibition led to bootlegging, speakeasies, and organized crime
The Harlem Renaissance was a flourishing of Black arts, literature, and music
Key Harlem Renaissance figures: Langston Hughes, Zora Neale Hurston, Louis Armstrong, Duke Ellington
The 1920s saw the rise of consumer culture, car ownership, and mass media
Radio, movies, and advertising created a shared national popular culture
Gender roles changed in the 1920s — flappers, more women in the workforce
Marcus Garvey and the UNIA promoted Black nationalism and pride
I know the causes of the Great Depression
Stock market speculation and the crash of October 1929 triggered the Depression
Overproduction in farming and industry were underlying economic weaknesses
Wealth was distributed very unequally in the 1920s, limiting most people's buying power
Weaknesses in the banking system led to widespread bank failures
The Great Depression caused mass unemployment, poverty, and homelessness
The Dust Bowl was an environmental disaster that made the Depression even worse on the Plains
President Hoover's response to the Depression was limited — he believed in voluntary cooperation
FDR won the 1932 election promising a 'New Deal' for Americans
Key New Deal programs included the CCC, TVA, WPA, AAA, FDIC, SEC, and Social Security
The Social Security Act (1935) created a safety net for the elderly, unemployed, and disabled
The Wagner Act protected workers' right to organize unions and bargain collectively
FDR's court-packing plan — trying to add justices to the Supreme Court — was controversial
New Deal critics included Huey Long (from the left), the Liberty League (from the right), and Father Coughlin
The New Deal permanently expanded the role and size of the federal government
The New Deal coalition brought together unions, Black voters, ethnic minorities, Southerners, and city voters
The New Deal didn't do enough for African Americans, women, and other marginalized groups
US foreign policy between the world wars was mostly isolationist
The Neutrality Acts of the 1930s tried to keep the US out of foreign wars
Fascism rose in Germany and Italy, and Japan became increasingly militaristic
The US gradually shifted from isolation to getting involved as WWII spread
The Lend-Lease Act sent military supplies to Britain and the Allies before the US officially entered the war
Japan attacked Pearl Harbor on December 7, 1941, bringing the US into WWII
I know how the US mobilized for World War II
The economy shifted from making consumer goods to producing war materials
Women entered the workforce in large numbers — symbolized by 'Rosie the Riveter'
The Double V Campaign called for victory over fascism abroad and racism at home
Japanese Americans were forced into internment camps during WWII — a major civil liberties violation
Korematsu v. US was the Supreme Court case that upheld Japanese American internment
I know the major military events and strategy of World War II
The Allied strategy in Europe went from North Africa to Italy to D-Day to defeating Nazi Germany
In the Pacific, the US used an island-hopping strategy to get closer to Japan
The US dropped atomic bombs on Hiroshima and Nagasaki in August 1945
People debated whether using atomic bombs was moral or necessary
Key wartime diplomacy included the Atlantic Charter, Yalta Conference, and Potsdam Conference
The United Nations was created as an international peacekeeping organization
Tensions between the US and Soviet Union at Yalta and Potsdam foreshadowed the Cold War
I know the origins of the Cold War between the US and the Soviet Union
The Truman Doctrine committed the US to stopping the spread of communism
The Marshall Plan gave massive economic aid to rebuild Western Europe
The Berlin Blockade led to the Berlin Airlift (1948-1949) to keep West Berlin supplied
NATO was formed as a military alliance to defend against Soviet expansion
Containment — stopping communism from spreading — was the foundation of US Cold War strategy
The Korean War (1950-1953) was the first major military conflict of the Cold War
The US and Soviet Union engaged in a nuclear arms race based on mutually assured destruction
The Cuban Missile Crisis (1962) was the closest the world came to nuclear war
The Space Race included Sputnik and the Apollo Moon landing
The Second Red Scare fueled anti-communist fears in postwar America
HUAC investigated suspected communists and created the Hollywood blacklist
Senator Joseph McCarthy led an aggressive anti-communist crusade in the early 1950s
McCarthyism restricted civil liberties through loyalty oaths and political investigations
The Army-McCarthy hearings led to McCarthy's downfall and censure
There was a massive postwar economic boom and unprecedented prosperity
Suburbs grew thanks to the GI Bill, FHA loans, and car culture
Levittown symbolized mass-produced suburban development
The baby boom and consumer culture defined the 1950s
Television became the dominant medium shaping American culture
The Interstate Highway Act (1956) transformed transportation and development across America
The Sunbelt migration shifted population and political power to the South and West
Suburban growth reinforced racial segregation and 'white flight' from cities
Early civil rights efforts began in the postwar era
President Truman desegregated the US military in 1948
The NAACP's strategy was to challenge segregation through lawsuits in the courts
Brown v. Board of Education (1954) ruled that school segregation was unconstitutional
There was massive resistance to desegregation, including the Little Rock Nine crisis (1957)
Cold War foreign policy continued to develop through the 1950s and 1960s
Eisenhower's foreign policies included massive retaliation, CIA covert ops, and the Eisenhower Doctrine
Kennedy's foreign policy included the Bay of Pigs, Cuban Missile Crisis, and Peace Corps
US involvement in Vietnam started gradually and then escalated
The domino theory said if one country fell to communism, its neighbors would follow
The Gulf of Tonkin Resolution gave the president broad war powers in Vietnam
The Vietnam War escalated under Presidents Johnson and Nixon
The Tet Offensive (1968) destroyed American public support for the Vietnam War
The antiwar movement grew through protests, draft resistance, and teach-ins
The Kent State shootings (1970) intensified antiwar sentiment
Nixon's Vietnamization policy gradually shifted combat responsibility to South Vietnamese forces
The Paris Peace Accords (1973) led to US withdrawal from Vietnam
Saigon fell in 1975, ending the Vietnam War
Vietnam had lasting impacts on US foreign policy, public trust, and led to the War Powers Act
President Johnson's Great Society programs expanded the federal government's domestic role
Medicare and Medicaid provided health insurance for elderly and low-income Americans
The War on Poverty included programs like Head Start, Job Corps, VISTA, and food stamps
The Immigration Act of 1965 abolished the old national origins quota system
The Civil Rights Act of 1964 and Voting Rights Act of 1965 were major Great Society achievements
The Montgomery Bus Boycott (1955-1956) was an early mass civil rights action
Martin Luther King Jr. led the nonviolent civil rights movement
Sit-ins, Freedom Rides, and other nonviolent direct actions were key civil rights tactics
The March on Washington (1963) featured King's 'I Have a Dream' speech
The Birmingham Campaign (1963) confronted Bull Connor's brutal tactics
The Civil Rights Act of 1964 banned discrimination in public places and employment
The Voting Rights Act of 1965 outlawed discriminatory voting practices like literacy tests
The 24th Amendment abolished the poll tax in federal elections
The movement shifted toward Black Power and more assertive approaches to racial justice
Malcolm X and the Nation of Islam advocated for Black self-determination
The Black Panther Party pushed for armed self-defense and ran community programs
Urban riots in Watts, Detroit, and Newark reflected deep frustration with systemic racism
Martin Luther King Jr. was assassinated in April 1968
The civil rights movement inspired other social justice movements
The feminist/women's liberation movement and the National Organization for Women pushed for equality
The Equal Rights Amendment campaign ultimately failed to get ratified
Betty Friedan's 'The Feminine Mystique' helped launch the second-wave feminist movement
Title IX banned sex discrimination in federally funded education programs
Roe v. Wade (1973) established the constitutional right to abortion
The Chicano movement fought for Mexican American civil rights
César Chávez and the United Farm Workers organized migrant farm laborers
The American Indian Movement fought for Native American rights and sovereignty
The Stonewall Riots (1969) sparked the modern gay rights movement
The Asian American movement fought for civil rights and cultural recognition
1960s youth counterculture rejected mainstream American values
The counterculture emphasized personal freedom, experimentation, and anti-establishment views
Rock music, Woodstock, and the hippie movement had a big cultural impact
The environmental movement emerged in the 1960s and 1970s
Rachel Carson's 'Silent Spring' sparked modern environmental awareness
Nixon created the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) in 1970
Key environmental laws included the Clean Air Act, Clean Water Act, and Endangered Species Act
The first Earth Day (1970) showed growing public concern for the environment
Americans experienced a crisis of confidence in government during the 1970s
The Watergate scandal forced President Nixon to resign in August 1974
Watergate damaged public trust in government and presidential power
Stagflation meant the economy was stagnant with high inflation and unemployment at the same time
The 1973 OPEC oil embargo caused an energy crisis and economic disruption
Nixon achieved détente with the Soviet Union and opened relations with China
SALT treaties were arms control agreements that limited nuclear weapons
Carter's presidency included the Camp David Accords, human rights focus, Iran Hostage Crisis, and energy crisis
The Iran Hostage Crisis (1979-1981) symbolized American weakness and fueled the rise of conservatism
The modern conservative movement rose to power in the late 20th century
The Moral Majority and the Religious Right became powerful political forces
Conservatives opposed the Great Society, high taxes, government regulation, and social liberalism
Reagan's election in 1980 was a major shift toward conservative politics
Reaganomics meant supply-side tax cuts, deregulation, and cutting domestic spending
Supply-side economics argued that cutting taxes for the wealthy would benefit everyone
The national debt increased significantly under Reagan
Reagan massively built up the military and used tough anti-Soviet rhetoric
The Strategic Defense Initiative (SDI or 'Star Wars') was a proposed missile defense system
Conservative judicial appointments shifted federal courts to the right
I know how and why the Cold War ended
Gorbachev's reforms (glasnost and perestroika) weakened Soviet control
The Berlin Wall fell in November 1989, symbolizing the end of the Cold War
The Soviet Union dissolved in December 1991
The US became the world's sole superpower after the Cold War ended
The US faced new foreign policy challenges after the Cold War
The Persian Gulf War (1991) pushed Iraq out of Kuwait
The US intervened in Somalia, Bosnia, and Kosovo for humanitarian and military reasons
Major economic transformations happened in the late 20th and early 21st centuries
Globalization and free trade agreements like NAFTA had significant effects on the economy
The internet and technology revolution transformed the American economy
The economy shifted from manufacturing to service and information-based industries
Income inequality grew and people debated economic policy and taxation
The 2008 financial crisis caused the Great Recession, and the government stepped in to respond
Immigration and demographics in America have changed a lot since 1980
Immigration from Latin America and Asia reshaped American demographics
Debates continue over immigration policy, border security, and undocumented immigration
The US has become more racially and ethnically diverse
There are ongoing debates about national identity, multiculturalism, and cultural change
The September 11, 2001 terrorist attacks hit the United States
The War on Terror led to the US invasion of Afghanistan in 2001
The Iraq War began in 2003, and its justification was heavily debated
The USA PATRIOT Act raised debates about balancing civil liberties with national security
The Department of Homeland Security was created after 9/11
Political polarization and partisan division have been increasing in American politics
Debates continue over the government's role in healthcare, the environment, and the economy
The 'culture wars' involve fights over abortion, gun control, and LGBTQ+ rights
The Iran-Contra Affair was a foreign policy scandal during the Reagan era
